1 

Product Identification and Software Version 

This document describes the specification of one of the data products generated by the OCO-2 Science Data Operations System (SDOS).  This document applies to the 
following product and system version: 

Product Name: 
Short Name: 
SDOS System Version: 
GES DISC Version: 
Product Description: 

Level 1B Product 
OCO2_L1B_Science, OCO2_L1B_Calibration 
B7.3.00 and later 
Version 7 and Version 7r 
Geolocated, radiometrically calibrated data in a standard granularity (for both science and calibration observations) 

SDOS System Bn.x.yy generates products either with predictive calibration parameters (Version n) or with parameters derived via analysis of past instrument performance (Version nr).  
Therefore, nr is considered of higher quality.  Analyses mixing the two sets should be undertaken with a degree of caution.  The product specifications for n and nr are identical and this 
document applies to both.   

2 

Background information 

The  OCO-2  SDOS  converts  telemetry  downloaded  from  the  Observatory  into  data  products  that  provide  comprehensive  mission  results  as  well  as  material  for  further 
research and investigation. The SDOS generates products from Level 0 through Level 2, some of which are available for distribution to both the scientific community and 
the general public.  All products are available to users of the SDOS computing cluster.  

2.1  NASA Data Levels 

The following table provides the definitions of the data levels used in this document.  These definitions are standard within the NASA community 

Level 
Packet data 
Level 0 

Level 1A 

Level 1B 

Level 2 

Description 
Telemetry data stream as received at the ground station, with science and engineering data embedded 
Instrument  science  data  (e.g.,  raw  voltages,  counts)  at  full  resolution,  time  ordered,  with  duplicates  and  transmission 
errors removed 
NASA Level 0 data that have been located in space and may have been transformed (e.g., calibrated, rearranged) in a 
reversible manner and packaged with needed ancillary and auxiliary data (e.g., radiances with the calibration equations 
applied) 
Irreversibly  transformed  (e.g.,  resampled,  remapped,  calibrated)  values  of  the  instrument  measurements  (e.g., 
radiances, magnetic field strength) 
Geophysical parameters, generally derived from NASA Level 1 data, and located in space and time commensurate with 
instrument location, pointing, and sampling

Product Description 

4.1  Format and Size 

This product is in HDF-5 format.  For most nominal orbits, the Science products use 1.1 Gigabytes (potentially across multiple files) and the Calibration products use 
between 35 and 77 Megabytes.

 7  Appendix 1:  Shape Descriptions 

The shape name of a data element is a descriptive label that describes the rank and dimensions of that element.  

Rules for creating shapes: 

1.  Shape names do not include any context information, such as what mode the instrument is in when it takes data with that shape.  Any context information needed to distinguish between 

similarly named dimensions is appended as a label, just before the "_Array" suffix. 

If Frame and Sounding are both present, they occur in direct sequence, i.e. Frame_Sounding_. 

2.  Any "temporal" dimension, e.g. Frame, is always outermost. 
3. 
4.  Shapes that include Frame, Sounding, and Spectrum cannot have any additional dimensions. 
5.  Spectrum precedes all other physical instrument dimensions, except when this rule contradicts any of the above rules. 
6. 
7.  Color comes after SinglePixel. 
8.  SinglePixel comes after Slice. 

If Spectrum and Sounding are present in the absence of Frame, they occur in direct sequence, i.e., Spectrum_Sounding.
